findings.finding: The plan asserts that 'complexity/clone/coverage trends' are snapshot-backed metrics that rebar metrics will surface (stating they appear as 'unavailable' until backfilled). No complexity, clone, or coverage metric is registered in the REGISTRY anywhere in src/rebar/metrics/. The github_actions.py coverage adapter explicitly registers nothing into REGISTRY. The planned janitor skill instruction — and the user-guide section — would direct agents and users to look for metric values that do not exist in any form.
findings.location: ## Why (paragraph 2: 'snapshot-backed complexity/clone/coverage trends'); ## Files → examples/agent-skills/rebar-janitor/phases/discovery.md ('complexity/clone/coverage are snapshot-backed and may read `unavailable` until backfilled')
scenarios: A janitor subagent runs `rebar metrics --output json` and tries to cite complexity/clone/coverage values — every entry for these will be absent (not even 'unavailable'), causing the agent to either hallucinate values or report confusion.
A user reads the 'Metrics' section of user-guide.md expecting to find complexity/clone/coverage documented as available lenses — they don't exist in any lens.

The discovery.md guidance tells agents these metrics 'may read unavailable until backfilled', implying they will eventually appear — but there is no registered spec for them, so they will never appear.
findings.suggested_fix: In the Why section and the Files/discovery.md guidance, remove all references to 'complexity/clone/coverage' as rebar metrics values. The plan should confine the janitor's `rebar metrics` citation to the metrics that actually exist: module-size distribution (module_size_distribution / module_size_trend / oversized_module_count), churn, refactor_to_addition_ratio, cap_change_events (code_health lens), the agent_process metrics (attempts_per_ticket, rework_within_7_days, revert_recovery, reopen_recovery, first_pass_rate), and gate_economics metrics.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Remove or qualify the complexity/clone/coverage claim in the `discovery.md` guidance — those MetricSpecs do not exist in the current REGISTRY and will produce no output rows (not even `unavailable`); restrict the cited code-health lens metrics to those actually registered: `module_size_distribution`, `oversized_module_count`, `churn`, and `refactor_to_addition_ratio`.

ABSENCE: no MetricSpec with lens or id containing 'complexity', 'clone', or 'coverage' exists in src/rebar/metrics/registry.py, git_metrics.py, event_metrics.py, or sidecar_metrics.py

The `rebar metrics` command iterates only over REGISTRY entries (metrics.py line 130: `for spec in rebar.metrics.registry.REGISTRY`), so absent specs produce no output row at all — not an `unavailable` row
findings.finding: The discovery.md skill guidance (Files section) instructs agents that 'complexity/clone/coverage are snapshot-backed and may read `unavailable` until backfilled,' but no complexity, clone, or coverage MetricSpecs exist in the actual REGISTRY (registry.py, git_metrics.py, event_metrics.py, sidecar_metrics.py were all checked). These metric IDs will not appear in `rebar metrics --output json` output at all — not even as `unavailable` entries — so an agent following the skill will search for fields that are simply absent, producing misplaced effort or confusion.
findings.location: Files section → examples/agent-skills/rebar-janitor/phases/discovery.md description; also the Why section ('complexity/clone/coverage trends where those snapshots have accrued')
scenarios: A janitor subagent running `rebar metrics --output json` looks for complexity/clone/coverage keys in the JSON and finds none — it may incorrectly infer the command failed, the metrics are missing, or the repo is not a rebar store.

The skill note 'may read unavailable until backfilled' implies the keys will appear with an unavailable sentinel; they will not appear at all, so the agent's parsing logic for 'unavailable' will never match.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'complexity/clone/coverage are snapshot-backed and may read `unavailable` until backfilled' with 'additional metrics (e.g. complexity, clone, coverage) are not yet registered; omit them from evidence rather than treating absence as zero.'

findings.finding: The plan asserts that 'complexity/clone/coverage trends where those snapshots have accrued (otherwise `unavailable`)' are part of the `rebar metrics` output, implying these metrics exist in the REGISTRY but report `unavailable` until backfilled. The actual REGISTRY (registry.py + all three reader-module registrations: event_metrics.py, git_metrics.py, sidecar_metrics.py) has no complexity, clone, or coverage metric specs at all — they would not appear in `rebar metrics` output even as `unavailable`. A janitor agent following the skill guidance and looking for these entries in JSON output will find them absent (not `unavailable`), undermining the cited-evidence model.
findings.location: Files section (discovery.md description) and Why section: 'complexity/clone/coverage trends where those snapshots have accrued (otherwise `unavailable`)'
scenarios: A janitor subagent follows the discovery.md skill guidance and issues `rebar metrics --output json`; the JSON output has no complexity/clone/coverage keys at all (not even `unavailable` entries), so the agent either hallucinates values or reports a confusing absence that was described as merely 'not yet accrued'.

The user-guide Metrics section (docs/user-guide.md) documents complexity/clone/coverage as snapshot-backed lenses users can expect to see, leading to support confusion when the command never emits them.
findings.suggested_fix: Remove 'complexity/clone/coverage' from both the discovery.md guidance and the user-guide Metrics section description (or replace with a future-tense caveat such as 'complexity/clone/coverage specs are planned but not yet registered; they will not appear in current output'). The four confirmed code-health metrics (module_size_distribution, oversized_module_count, churn, refactor_to_addition_ratio) plus the delivery and gate_economics lenses are sufficient evidence for the janitor use-case.

findings.finding: The plan instructs the rebar-janitor discovery skill to cite 'complexity/clone/coverage' metrics from `rebar metrics` output — but no complexity, clone, or coverage metrics exist anywhere in the REGISTRY. The instruction will cause the discovery subagent to look for metric IDs that are simply absent from the JSON output, creating precisely the 'treat absence as zero' failure mode the plan warns against.
findings.location: Files section (discovery.md description): 'complexity/clone/coverage are snapshot-backed and may read `unavailable` until backfilled'; also Why section: 'snapshot-backed complexity/clone/coverage trends where those snapshots have accrued'
scenarios: A discovery subagent follows the skill instructions and scans the `rebar metrics` JSON for complexity/clone/coverage keys, finds them absent (not even as `unavailable` entries since the metric IDs aren't registered), and either hallucinates values or reports confusing missing-key errors.

The skill author's intent was to name these as `unavailable` sentinel signals, but since they're not registered, `rebar metrics` emits zero entries for them — the agent cannot distinguish 'metric registered but no data' from 'metric doesn't exist.'
findings.suggested_fix: In the Files/discovery.md description and Why section, replace 'complexity/clone/coverage are snapshot-backed and may read `unavailable` until backfilled' with the actual registered code_health metrics: module_size_distribution (distribution dict), oversized_module_count (integer), churn (insertions/deletions dict), refactor_to_addition_ratio (float or unavailable). These are the real durable signals the skill should cite.

src/rebar/metrics/git_metrics.py registers: `module_size_distribution`, `churn`, `refactor_to_addition_ratio`, `cap_change_events` — no complexity/clone/coverage
src/rebar/metrics/event_metrics.py registers: `attempts_per_ticket`, `rework_within_7_days`, `revert_recovery`, `reopen_recovery`, `first_pass_rate` — no complexity/clone/coverage

Evidence class: Contradicted — the environment (actual registry) falsifies the plan's claim that these metrics will appear as `unavailable`
findings.finding: The plan's `discovery.md` guidance asserts that 'complexity/clone/coverage are snapshot-backed and may read `unavailable` until backfilled,' implying an agent will see `unavailable` entries for those IDs in `rebar metrics --output json`. But no MetricSpec with IDs for complexity, clone, or coverage is registered in the actual REGISTRY (verified across all three registration modules: event_metrics.py, git_metrics.py, sidecar_metrics.py, and the seed specs in registry.py). Unregistered metrics produce no JSON output at all — not even an `unavailable` entry — so an agent following this guidance will find nothing where it expects something.
findings.location: Files section — `examples/agent-skills/rebar-janitor/phases/discovery.md` guidance; also implicit in the 'Why' section ('snapshot-backed complexity/clone/coverage trends where those snapshots have accrued')
scenarios: A janitor subagent runs `rebar metrics --output json` and parses the result looking for complexity, clone, or coverage entries — finds none, causing confusion about whether the command failed or whether the plan's caveat applies.
A future contributor reads discovery.md and believes complexity/clone/coverage metrics exist in the registry, and attempts to use them for evidence in a finding — producing a hallucinated citation.
findings.suggested_fix: In discovery.md and the Why section, replace the complexity/clone/coverage claim with the actual registered metric IDs that currently produce output (module_size_distribution, oversized_module_count, churn, refactor_to_addition_ratio for code_health; agent_process and gate_economics IDs for the other lenses). If complexity/clone/coverage are genuinely planned for future registration, frame them as 'not yet registered' rather than 'snapshot-backed and `unavailable`' — because `unavailable` implies the metric ID appears in the JSON output, which it will not until the MetricSpec is added to REGISTRY.

evidence: 2 criterion line(s) bundle multiple deliverables with 'and' (split so each is independently verifiable).
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either extend parent epic 86ac to explicitly include a 'Discoverability' track (updating its What, Decomposition, and ACs to cover the janitor skill, user-guide Metrics section, AGENTS.md pointer, and reuse-surface.md reference) and re-review the parent, or re-parent this leaf as a standalone follow-on task outside the 86ac epic.
evidence: Parent epic 86ac Decomposition: 'Three tracks; each child is a single concern: Command: metric-registry … Instrumentation: the caused_by link … Backfill (one-time, labeled)' — no discoverability/docs/skill track.

Parent epic 86ac ACs: five criteria covering command output, is_authoritative(), bug-close friction, and backfill labeling — none mention user-guide section, AGENTS.md pointer, reuse-surface.md, or janitor skill.
Leaf plan rationale explicitly frames this as a new concern: 'metrics no agent knows to read provide no value, so discoverability (docs) is part of "done"' — a rationale absent from the parent.
Leaf Files section targets: examples/agent-skills/rebar-janitor/phases/discovery.md, docs/user-guide.md, docs/reuse-surface.md, AGENTS.md — none referenced in parent epic.
findings.finding: The leaf delivers four items (rebar-janitor skill update, docs/user-guide.md Metrics section, docs/reuse-surface.md registry reference, AGENTS.md pointer) that do not appear in the parent epic's declared scope. The parent's Decomposition explicitly names exactly three tracks — Command, Instrumentation, Backfill — none of which encompasses documentation discoverability or agent-skill teaching. None of the parent's five Acceptance Criteria mention these deliverables. The leaf introduces a wholly new concern ('discoverability') that the parent does not contain.
findings.location: Leaf: What / Files / Acceptance Criteria; Parent (86ac): Decomposition (three tracks) and ACs
scenarios: An implementer closing all three parent-track children would consider the epic complete but these four leaf deliverables would remain unaccounted for under the parent's contract.
The parent's rollup AC ('rebar list --epic <this> --status open returning none') can pass without any of the leaf's four files being touched, because the parent's ACs don't require them.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a fourth track to the parent epic's Decomposition: 'Discoverability: teach rebar-janitor to consult rebar metrics (discovery.md); document the command for human contributors and agents (docs/user-guide.md Metrics section, AGENTS.md pointer, docs/reuse-surface.md registry reference).' Then add a corresponding AC to the parent: 'docs/user-guide.md contains a Metrics section naming the four lenses; AGENTS.md has a metrics pointer; docs/reuse-surface.md references rebar.metrics; discovery.md instructs the subagent to run rebar metrics.' Update the parent first (which stales its plan-review attestation), then re-review this leaf against the corrected parent.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add to discovery.md and user-guide.md explicit guidance for the two non-happy-path states: (a) `unavailable` metrics → treat as absent evidence and rely on the cloc/tokei pass, note reason in findings; (b) command failure (non-zero exit / malformed JSON) → log the error, skip the metrics step, and continue with one-shot tools — so agents and contributors have a clear fallback rather than an undefined branch.
evidence: Plan says discovery.md should 'Note the `unavailable` state so the agent does not treat absence as zero' — this prevents one misinterpretation but gives no fallback path
Plan says user-guide.md should document the `unavailable{reason,accruing_since}` state — documents the structure but not what the reader should do next
ABSENCE: no mention of command failure handling (non-zero exit, malformed JSON) in either deliverable spec
findings.finding: The user-guide.md Metrics section spec and the discovery.md skill guidance both mention the `unavailable` state but neither specifies the recommended next action for a user or agent who encounters it — the plan treats `unavailable` as a state to document but not a branching point with actionable guidance (e.g., 'if all code_health metrics are unavailable, note this in findings and rely solely on the cloc/tokei pass'). Similarly, discovery.md guidance does not address what the agent should do if the `rebar metrics` command itself fails (non-zero exit, malformed/empty JSON output).
findings.location: Files section — `examples/agent-skills/rebar-janitor/phases/discovery.md` and `docs/user-guide.md`; AC lines 1 and 2
scenarios: An agent runs `rebar metrics` and gets a non-zero exit code (e.g., not a rebar store despite the guard, CLI bug) — with no fallback specified, the agent may stall, error out, or silently skip metrics
A contributor reads the user-guide.md Metrics section, sees all lenses as `unavailable`, and has no guidance on whether to wait, backfill, or proceed without metrics
An agent interprets widespread `unavailable` as a data problem and loops retrying rather than falling back to the cloc/tokei evidence already in hand
findings.suggested_fix: In discovery.md, after the instruction to run `rebar metrics`, add: 'If the command exits non-zero or returns malformed JSON, skip this step and rely on the cloc/tokei pass; note the failure in the Discovery summary. If individual metric values are `unavailable`, cite the `accruing_since` date if present and treat that lens as no evidence (not zero).' Mirror this as a short paragraph in the user-guide.md Metrics section under a 'When metrics are unavailable' sub-heading.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a fallback rule to the discovery.md spec for when `rebar metrics` fails or the repo is not a rebar store (e.g., 'if the command is unavailable or exits non-zero, skip this step and note the absence in the evidence pool — do not block the discovery phase').
evidence: Plan (Files/discovery.md): 'a subagent should run `rebar metrics --since <date> --until <date> --output json` (when the repo is a rebar store)'
Plan specifies the `unavailable` state caveat but only for metric-level unavailability, not command-level failure
No fallback protocol is given for: command not found, non-rebar repo, non-zero exit, or partial JSON output

The existing discovery.md Phase 0 pattern sets a precedent for tool-availability fallbacks ('Confirm whether ast-grep, semgrep, cloc/tokei are installed. If not... Don't block — fall back to rg/grep')
findings.finding: The plan specifies that the discovery subagent should note the `unavailable` state 'so the agent does not treat absence as zero,' but provides no decision rule / fallback for when the `rebar metrics` command itself fails entirely (non-zero exit, not a rebar store, command not found). The only conditional given is 'when the repo is a rebar store' — but no protocol is specified for what the subagent does when the command errors out.
findings.location: Files section (discovery.md description): 'when the repo is a rebar store' qualifier; AC1: 'instructs a Discovery subagent to consult `rebar metrics`'
scenarios: The repo being audited is not a rebar store — `rebar metrics` exits non-zero; the subagent has no instruction on whether to skip, report the error as a finding, or continue.
The `rebar` CLI is not installed in the audit environment; the subagent blocks waiting for a tool that will never succeed.
findings.suggested_fix: Mirror the existing ast-grep/tokei fallback pattern in discovery.md Phase 0: 'If `rebar` is not installed or the command returns non-zero (not a rebar store), skip this step and record "rebar metrics unavailable" in the evidence pool — do not block.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the default test location in the Tests section (e.g. 'add a new `tests/scripts/check_metrics_docs.sh` grep test') so the executor does not have to choose.
evidence: 'These can be a lightweight tests/scripts/-tier grep test or folded into the existing docs-index/README drift checks'
findings.finding: The Tests section defers the location of the grep/assertion tests to the executor with an 'or' choice — 'a lightweight tests/scripts/-tier grep test or folded into the existing docs-index/README drift checks' — without stating a default. An executing agent must decide where to put the tests, which is a real design choice (separate file vs modifying existing gates).
scenarios: An executor adds a new standalone script when the convention is to fold into existing drift checks, or vice versa, causing drift from project conventions and a potential review dispute.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the 'or' with a stated default: 'Add a new `tests/scripts/check_metrics_docs.sh` (or equivalent) that greps for the required strings; folding into existing drift checks is acceptable only if the project already has a docs-string-presence gate there.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Anchor each AC's grep pattern concretely (e.g. AC1: `grep -E 'rebar metrics.*--since'`; AC2: `grep -E '^## Metrics'` plus `grep -E 'agent_process|code_health|delivery|gate_economics'`; AC3: `grep -E 'Metrics.*docs/'`; AC4: `grep -qF 'rebar.metrics'`) to eliminate substring false-positives.
evidence: AC1: 'verified by a grep/test asserting the `rebar metrics` reference and the `unavailable`-state caveat are present'
AC2: 'asserted by a grep/test for the section heading and the lens names'
No anchored grep pattern (e.g. grep -E '^## Metrics') is given for any AC
findings.finding: The grep-based proving commands for ACs 1–4 are described in prose without anchored patterns, creating a substring false-positive risk (VERIFY-COMMAND DEFECT TAXONOMY item 1). For example, AC1's 'grep/test asserting the `rebar metrics` reference' would pass if discovery.md already mentions 'rebar metrics' in any context (e.g. a comment saying 'do not use rebar metrics here'), and AC2's 'grep/test for the section heading' does not specify the required heading text.
scenarios: A pre-existing mention of 'rebar metrics' in discovery.md causes AC1 to pass without the required instructional content being added.
A heading mismatch (e.g. '### Metrics' vs '## Metrics') passes the grep but renders the section invisible in rendered docs.
findings.suggested_fix: For each AC, replace the prose description with a one-line example anchored grep: AC1 `grep -E 'rebar metrics --since'` + `grep -F 'unavailable'`; AC2 `grep -E '^#{1,3} Metrics'` + `grep -cE 'agent_process|code_health|delivery|gate_economics'`; AC3 `grep -E 'Metrics.*docs/user-guide'`; AC4 `grep -F 'rebar.metrics'`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `tests/unit/test_reuse_surface_doc.py` to the Files list and extend it (or note explicitly that the rebar.metrics section intentionally omits callable-level anti-drift coverage and why) so the doc's stated invariant — 'every signature below is CI-verified' — remains true after the edit.
evidence: docs/reuse-surface.md header: 'Every signature below is verified against the current source by tests/unit/test_reuse_surface_doc.py (a CI anti-drift gate that introspects each documented callable)'
tests/unit/test_reuse_surface_doc.py currently covers only signing, LLM runner, prompt library, and workflow executor — no rebar.metrics assertions
Plan's Files section does not list tests/unit/test_reuse_surface_doc.py as a file to update
Plan's Tests section specifies only grep-level presence checks, not signature anti-drift assertions for the new rebar.metrics content
findings.finding: The plan adds rebar.metrics signature/API content to docs/reuse-surface.md but does not enumerate tests/unit/test_reuse_surface_doc.py as a file requiring updating, even though that file is the CI anti-drift gate whose coverage promise is stated in the doc's own header ('Every signature below is verified against the current source by tests/unit/test_reuse_surface_doc.py'). Adding metrics API content to reuse-surface.md without extending the anti-drift test breaks the doc's stated invariant.
scenarios: A future change renames MetricSpec fields or is_authoritative's signature; test_reuse_surface_doc.py does not catch the drift because the plan never wired it to cover rebar.metrics — the doc silently lies about being CI-verified.
A reviewer or agent reads reuse-surface.md trusting its header guarantee and relies on a stale signature that was never anti-drift tested.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a test case to tests/unit/test_reuse_surface_doc.py that introspects the rebar.metrics symbols documented in reuse-surface.md (e.g. asserting MetricSpec fields, is_authoritative signature, REGISTRY type), mirroring the pattern of the existing test cases.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `docs/README.md` to the Files list with a one-word update to the reuse-surface.md blurb (e.g. append 'and the metrics registry') so the docs index accurately reflects the new content.
evidence: docs/README.md line 146-147: '**[reuse-surface.md](reuse-surface.md)** — the developer API reference for the reusable subsystems (signing, LLM runtime, prompt/contract, output-schema seams).'
The plan adds rebar.metrics as a fifth reusable subsystem to reuse-surface.md but does not list docs/README.md as a file to update
findings.finding: The plan edits docs/reuse-surface.md to reference the rebar.metrics subsystem, but docs/README.md's blurb for reuse-surface.md is an unenumerated consumer that describes the file as covering only 'signing, LLM runtime, prompt/contract, output-schema seams' — the blurb would become stale/misleading without a corresponding update.
scenarios: An agent or contributor reads docs/README.md to understand what reuse-surface.md covers and does not know to look there for the metrics registry API, defeating the discoverability goal of the plan.
findings.suggested_fix: In docs/README.md, update the reuse-surface.md entry to: '…(signing, LLM runtime, prompt/contract, output-schema seams, and the metrics registry)'.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
